title: Baked Salmon with Spring Pea Puree: Tender and Vibrant Weeknight Dinner
promptIMG: Professional food photography prompt: "baked salmon fillet on white plate with vibrant green spring pea puree, natural food photography, balanced sharp focus, zero blur, realistic textures of flaky fish and smooth puree, modern kitchen background in sharp focus, natural window lighting, kitchen countertop visible, documentary food photography style, appetizing presentation --no blur, depth of field, bokeh, over-sharpening, artificial enhancement --quality 2 --ar 4:3 --stylize 300"
description: This recipe for baked salmon paired with a fresh spring pea puree combines gentle cooking techniques to preserve the fish’s moisture and texture, while the puree adds a bright, natural sweetness. Learn how to balance timing and temperature to achieve flaky, tender salmon and a smooth, vibrant puree that complements it perfectly.
yield: 4 salmon fillets with spring pea puree
ingredients: 4 salmon fillets (about 150g / 5 oz each), skin on or off as preferred
Salt and freshly ground black pepper, to taste
250g (9 oz) fresh or frozen shelled peas
1 tbsp (15ml) unsalted butter (or olive oil for dairy-free)
Fresh mint leaves (optional, about 5g) for puree
Salt and pepper to taste for puree
introduction: I often turn to baked salmon when I want a simple yet satisfying dinner that doesn’t require much fuss. This version stands out because of the spring pea puree, which brings a fresh, subtly sweet contrast to the rich salmon. I first made this dish during early spring when fresh peas were at their peak, and the vibrant green puree brightened the plate and the meal. The salmon stays moist and tender thanks to gentle baking, while the puree’s smooth texture and light acidity balance the dish beautifully. It’s a great option for weeknights or a light weekend meal, especially when you want something colorful and nourishing without spending hours in the kitchen.

why_this_recipe_works.section: Understanding the Cooking Techniques Behind This Baked Salmon with Spring Pea Puree
why_this_recipe_works.content:
Baking salmon gently at a moderate temperature ensures the fish cooks evenly without drying out. The olive oil helps retain moisture and encourages a subtle browning on the surface, enhancing flavor and texture. Cooking the salmon skin-on can add an extra layer of crispness and flavor, but skinless works equally well if preferred.

The spring pea puree is made by briefly cooking peas with aromatics and then blending them with broth and butter or oil. This method preserves the peas’ natural sweetness and vibrant color. Adding lemon juice brightens the puree, balancing the richness of the salmon. The butter or oil adds silkiness to the puree’s texture, making it smooth and luscious without heaviness.

Timing is essential: the peas cook quickly to maintain their bright green hue and fresh flavor, while the salmon’s baking time is short enough to keep it flaky and tender. Combining these elements results in a harmonious dish that highlights the best qualities of each ingredient.

ingredient_notes.salmon fillets: Choose fresh, high-quality salmon with a vibrant pink color and firm flesh. Skin-on fillets offer extra flavor and texture, but skinless is fine for those who prefer it. Thicker fillets require slightly longer baking times to cook through evenly.
ingredient_notes.fresh or frozen peas: Fresh peas provide the brightest flavor and color in the puree, but frozen peas are a convenient alternative that still maintain sweetness and texture when cooked properly. Avoid overcooking to prevent dull color and mushy texture.
ingredient_notes.shallot: Shallots add a subtle onion flavor without overpowering the peas. Finely chopping and gently sweating them enhances their sweetness and integrates their flavor smoothly into the puree.
ingredient_notes.lemon juice: Fresh lemon juice adds acidity that balances the richness of the salmon and butter in the puree. Adding it at the end preserves its bright, fresh notes rather than cooking it away.

instructions.instruction: Preheat your oven to 180°C (350°F). Line a baking tray with parchment paper or lightly grease it with olive oil.
instructions.doneness_cue: Oven reaches stable temperature; baking tray is prepared to prevent sticking.

instructions.instruction: Pat the salmon fillets dry with paper towels. Brush both sides lightly with olive oil, then season generously with salt and freshly ground black pepper.
instructions.doneness_cue: Salmon has a light sheen from oil and visible seasoning on the surface.

instructions.instruction: Place the salmon fillets on the prepared baking tray, skin-side down if using skin-on. Transfer to the oven and bake for 12-15 minutes, depending on thickness. The salmon is done when it flakes easily with a fork and the flesh turns opaque but remains moist.
instructions.doneness_cue: Salmon flesh changes from translucent to opaque; edges may slightly separate when tested with a fork.

instructions.instruction: While the salmon bakes, heat a small saucepan over medium heat and melt the butter. Add the chopped shallot and sauté gently for 2-3 minutes until softened and fragrant but not browned.
instructions.doneness_cue: Shallots become translucent and release a mild sweet aroma.

instructions.instruction: Add the peas and broth to the saucepan. Bring to a simmer and cook for 3-4 minutes until peas are just tender and the broth has slightly reduced. Avoid overcooking to preserve color and sweetness.
instructions.doneness_cue: Peas turn bright green and are tender but not mushy; broth slightly thickened.

instructions.instruction: Transfer the pea mixture to a blender or use an immersion blender. Add lemon juice and, if using, fresh mint leaves. Blend until smooth and creamy. Season to taste with salt and pepper.
instructions.doneness_cue: Puree is smooth with a vibrant green color and fresh aroma from lemon and mint.

instructions.instruction: Serve the baked salmon fillets on plates with a generous spoonful of spring pea puree alongside. Optionally garnish with extra mint leaves or lemon wedges.
instructions.doneness_cue: Warm salmon paired with cool, smooth puree; colors contrast attractively on the plate.

pro_tips.tip: Dry the salmon thoroughly before seasoning to promote better browning and prevent steaming.
pro_tips.reason: Moisture on the surface creates steam and inhibits the formation of a flavorful crust, resulting in a less appealing texture.

pro_tips.tip: Use a gentle sauté for shallots to avoid bitterness and maintain their natural sweetness.
pro_tips.reason: Overcooked shallots become bitter and overpower the delicate flavor of the pea puree.

pro_tips.tip: Avoid overcooking the peas; they should retain a bright green color and slight firmness.
pro_tips.reason: Overcooked peas lose their vibrant color and develop a dull, mushy texture, diminishing the puree’s appeal.

pro_tips.tip: Add lemon juice after blending to preserve its fresh, bright flavor.
pro_tips.reason: Cooking lemon juice reduces its acidity and brightness, making the puree less lively.

pro_tips.tip: Check salmon doneness by testing with a fork near the thickest part; it should flake easily but remain moist.
pro_tips.reason: Overbaked salmon becomes dry and tough, while underbaking leaves it raw and unpleasant.

common_mistakes_to_avoid.mistake: Overbaking the salmon causing dry, tough flesh.
common_mistakes_to_avoid.why_it_happens: High oven temperature or baking too long cooks out moisture, especially in thinner fillets.
common_mistakes_to_avoid.fix: Bake at moderate temperature (180°C/350°F) and check doneness at 12 minutes for average thickness. Remove promptly when opaque and flaky.

common_mistakes_to_avoid.mistake: Using frozen peas without thawing or rinsing, leading to watery puree.
common_mistakes_to_avoid.why_it_happens: Frozen peas contain ice crystals that release water when cooked, diluting the puree.
common_mistakes_to_avoid.fix: Thaw frozen peas and drain excess water before cooking to maintain puree consistency.

common_mistakes_to_avoid.mistake: Cooking peas too long, resulting in dull color and loss of sweetness.
common_mistakes_to_avoid.why_it_happens: Extended heat breaks down chlorophyll and sugars in peas.
common_mistakes_to_avoid.fix: Simmer peas briefly (3-4 minutes) until just tender, then blend immediately.

common_mistakes_to_avoid.mistake: Not seasoning the pea puree adequately, making it taste bland.
common_mistakes_to_avoid.why_it_happens: Purees need salt and acid to bring out natural flavors; skipping these steps leads to flat taste.
common_mistakes_to_avoid.fix: Season puree with salt and fresh lemon juice after blending; taste and adjust before serving.

variations.name: Dairy-Free Spring Pea Puree
variations.description: Replace butter with extra virgin olive oil or a mild-flavored nut oil to keep the puree creamy without dairy.
variations.technical_note: Olive oil adds richness and healthy fats but changes the mouthfeel slightly compared to butter, resulting in a lighter texture.

variations.name: Herb-Infused Pea Puree
variations.description: Add fresh herbs like basil, tarragon, or chives along with mint for different aromatic profiles.
variations.technical_note: Herbs should be added after cooking and blended to preserve their freshness and color.

variations.name: Garlic-Enhanced Pea Puree
variations.description: Sauté a small clove of minced garlic with shallots for a mild savory note in the puree.
variations.technical_note: Cook garlic gently to avoid bitterness; add with shallots and sauté briefly before adding peas.

variations.name: Citrus Zest Pea Puree
variations.description: Add finely grated lemon or lime zest to the puree for an extra layer of bright flavor.
variations.technical_note: Add zest after blending to maintain its aromatic oils and prevent bitterness.

variations.name: Salmon with Asparagus and Pea Puree
variations.description: Serve the salmon and pea puree alongside steamed or roasted asparagus for a spring-inspired plate.
variations.technical_note: Asparagus cooks quickly and pairs well texturally and flavor-wise with the pea puree and salmon.

nutrition.note: These values are estimates and may vary based on salmon portion size, oil amounts, and exact ingredients used.

storage_and_reheating.room_temperature: Do not leave cooked salmon or pea puree at room temperature for more than 2 hours to prevent bacterial growth.
storage_and_reheating.refrigerator: Store cooked salmon and pea puree separately in airtight containers for up to 2 days. Salmon texture may firm slightly but remains moist when reheated gently.
storage_and_reheating.freezer: Freezing is not recommended for best texture retention. If needed, freeze pea puree in airtight containers for up to 1 month; salmon may become dry after freezing and reheating.
storage_and_reheating.reheating: Reheat salmon gently in a low oven (around 120°C/250°F) for 10-15 minutes or in a covered pan on low heat. Warm pea puree in a small saucepan over low heat, stirring frequently to prevent sticking.
storage_and_reheating.make_ahead_strategy: Prepare the pea puree a day ahead and refrigerate. Bake the salmon fresh before serving to maintain optimal texture and flavor.

faq.question: Can I make the pea puree ahead of time?
faq.answer: Yes, the pea puree can be made up to 24 hours in advance and stored in the refrigerator in an airtight container. Reheat gently before serving.

faq.question: Why is my salmon dry after baking?
faq.answer: This usually happens if the salmon is baked at too high a temperature or for too long. Baking at 180°C (350°F) for 12-15 minutes and checking for flakiness helps keep it moist.

faq.question: Can I use frozen salmon fillets for this recipe?
faq.answer: You can, but thaw them completely and pat dry before seasoning and baking to ensure even cooking and prevent excess moisture.

faq.question: How long does leftover baked salmon last in the fridge?
faq.answer: Cooked salmon should be eaten within 2 days when stored properly in the refrigerator to ensure safety and quality.

faq.question: Can I substitute the peas with other vegetables for the puree?
faq.answer: Yes, fresh or frozen green beans or asparagus can be used, though the flavor and color will differ. Adjust cooking time to keep them tender but vibrant.

faq.question: Is it necessary to add lemon juice to the pea puree?
faq.answer: Lemon juice adds brightness and balances the richness of the butter and salmon, but you can omit or reduce it if you prefer a milder flavor.

conclusion: This baked salmon with spring pea puree offers a straightforward approach to combining delicate flavors and textures. I welcome your thoughts or any adjustments you've tried—sharing your experiences helps us all improve our cooking. Feel free to leave a comment below.
